title: ‘Superman’ Is Here to Save the Day. Are We Saved?
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins review James Gunn's 'Superman,' the first film of the rebooted DCU, then run through the Superman box-office history and the DC slate still to come.

films.title: Dune: Part Three
films.note: Officially real, titled 'Dune: Part Three' (not 'Dune Messiah'), with Timothée Chalamet, now in production and dated December 16, 2026, the same day as 'Avengers: Doomsday.' Sean and Amanda figure one of the two moves.

films.title: Superman
films.note: James Gunn's seventh feature and the first film of the rebooted DCU, drawn heavily from Grant Morrison's 'All-Star Superman.' David Corenswet plays a vulnerable, emo, early-30s Superman opposite Rachel Brosnahan's Lois Lane and Nicholas Hoult's Lex Luthor. Amanda, allergic to Gunn's joke-a-minute patter, still finds half of it works; Sean calls it a great Superman movie if not a perfect one, with a Saturday-morning-cartoon tone, brisk under two hours, and a Metropolis (shot in Cleveland) that feels real. They read the Luthor plot, old footage weaponized by internet troll armies against 'the last good man,' as Gunn re-litigating his 2018 Disney firing, and compare that self-regard to 'The Brutalist' and to Taylor Swift. Both flag the second-act pocket-universe/black-site detour as the low point: CGI space junk, a proton river, torture staged as an ICE parallel. The film's Baravia/Jarnapur war gestures at Israel/Palestine and Russia/Ukraine but lands on a Superman-is-good bromide. Corenswet and Brosnahan draw raves; the Justice Gang, Krypto, and the Gunn sci-fi goofiness draw sighs. Bradley Cooper cameos as Jor-El; Supergirl (Milly Alcock) and a stinger set up what's next.

films.title: Superman (1978)
films.note: Richard Donner's original, which both watched this week and which still casts a long shadow: the John Williams score, the suit, the hopefulness. Sean credits Gunn with echoing its spirit while replicating almost none of its style.

films.title: The Brutalist
films.note: Sean's mid-movie comparison: a great-man, main-character-energy story about an artist exiled and used by powerful forces, Brady Corbett pouring himself into it the way Gunn does here.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Cited as the rare comic-book movie with real ideas about isolationism, interventionism, and national identity, the company Gunn's 'Superman' is trying to keep when it reaches for geopolitics.

films.title: Batman v Superman: Dawn of Justice
films.note: The domestic Superman-movie record at $330 million, and per Sean, Amanda's favorite. Zack Snyder's Jesse Eisenberg Lex prefigured the evil-tech-billionaire type.

films.title: Man of Steel
films.note: $290 million. Sean thinks Henry Cavill struck the right figure but was failed by the writing, and played Superman with almost no humor.

films.title: Superman Returns
films.note: $200 million, with Brandon Routh and Kevin Spacey. A movie Amanda doesn't remember.

films.title: Superman II
films.note: $108 million. Sean is especially fond of the Donner Cut.

films.title: Superman IV: The Quest for Peace
films.note: The fiasco: $15 million. Sean predicts the new one opens to $150 million and could be the first Superman movie to clear a billion.

films.title: Supergirl
films.note: Dated June 2026, directed by Craig Gillespie ('I, Tonya,' 'Cruella'), with Milly Alcock (of 'House of the Dragon') teased at the end of 'Superman.' Amanda was annoyed by the found-family setup; both found Alcock and Corenswet's timing promising.

films.title: Clayface
films.note: Out September 11, a standalone horror movie about the Batman villain, a Hollywood actor who uses a special clay to transform. Written by Mike Flanagan, directed by James Watkins ('Speak No Evil'), starring Tom Rhys Harries. Likely a ~$50M movie, which Sean thinks is a cool swing for the third DCU film.

films.title: Swamp Thing
films.note: James Mangold is writing and directing a new one; Sean recalls the memorable-if-not-good Wes Craven 1982 version with Adrienne Barbeau.

films.title: The Batman Part II
films.note: Matt Reeves reportedly turned in a script Sean hears is very good, after a lot of deadline-shaming. Gunn played coy in Rolling Stone about whether Robert Pattinson's Batman and Corenswet's Superman ever share a screen; both hosts would watch it.

films.title: Sergeant Rock
films.note: The Luca Guadagnino DC project (a Captain America/Nick Fury-ish military figure), now in doubt after Daniel Craig exited.
